steps.title: Reading Velocity–Time Graphs
steps.content:
On a **velocity–time graph**:

- A **horizontal line** = constant velocity (zero acceleration)
- A **straight diagonal line** = constant acceleration
- A **curved line** = changing acceleration

The **gradient** = acceleration

The **area under the graph** = distance travelled

steps.title: Velocity–Time Graphs
steps.question: A velocity–time graph shows a straight line from v = 0 to v = 20 m/s over 5 seconds. What is the acceleration?
steps.correctFeedback: Correct! a = Δv/Δt = (20 − 0) / 5 = 4 m/s². The gradient of a v–t graph gives acceleration.
steps.misconceptionFeedback: Use a = Δv / Δt. Here Δv = 20 m/s and Δt = 5 s, so a = 20 ÷ 5 = 4 m/s². Don't confuse the final velocity (20 m/s) with the acceleration.
